speaker: interviewer
text: We're with Lucie Porges in New York City, and this is tape number four. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When the war ended, what-- what were the plans of-- of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents went back to Lyon, which was the last place where they were. And they didn't really know where to go from there. They knew they didn't want to go back to Vienna. Well, I had family in Israel. By that time, I had some family in America. And they could have possibly gone back to Belgium, but somehow it was not a good idea. We had-- We were cut off. And they just went back to Lyon to decide what else they were going to do. By that time, my sister and I were both in schools in Geneva, and we wanted to kind of finish school, and then possibly go somewhere with our parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that the Swiss were interested in getting rid of these refugees [INTERPOSING VOICES] as soon as they could. How was Lyon in-- in accepting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somehow I think the French accepted the people that were there before the war. It must have been all right. It wasn't a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though they weren't originally French.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even though-- yes, yes. They somehow accepted them. I don't know why and how, but they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have an occupation in Lyon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He did something with-- he worked with some jewelry and things, but I don't know exactly what he did. With some other Jewish people. He was involved with some other Jewish people, doing some business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were there many Jews with you in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Were there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jews with you in your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not too many. No. It was a Swiss art school And no, actually, I must say, we were, rather, a minority.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it wasn't a problem you staying in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a problem. No. We were very much into that programming. School was very interesting, and that was the major part of it. We were very involved in that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were your parents in Lyon?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were in Lyon for another few years. As a matter of fact, they were still in Lyon when I came to America to get married in 1951. And they only followed a few years later when my sister and I-- no, when I was in [INAUDIBLE]. My sister went to Israel after the war. So they came only a few years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they find out about the-- you said that they knew that they had rel-- they had family members in Vienna who were deported. How-- how did they find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They must have found out through the Jewish community. And mostly my uncle, who lived in Switzerland, probably found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they actively make an effort to-- to find these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure they did. I'm sure they did, but-- and I only realized last year, when I found my grandfather's grave in Vienna, that my uncle must have put this in 1947-- the stone. When he found out that my grandfather's ashes were sent back, he must have put that stone. But I discovered it only last year. They never talked to us about it. I was so surprised and touched to find this stone in Vienna at the Jewish cemetery, the part that is totally neglected. There's part of the cemetery that has beautiful stones and everything, from people who died before the war. But all the people that died afterwards, where the ashes were sent back-- they are just in some kind of a potter's field. And some have-- have nothing at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's speak about your grandparents and what their experience was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I went back to Vienna last year and tried to find out, I realized only then that, after we left in 1939 and my grandparents stayed alone in Vienna, my grandfather was sent to Buchenwald, just a few months after we left, in 1939. And after a few months, he died there. And they sent his ashes back to Vienna. At this point, apparently, my grandmother was still in Vienna till 1942, for another two years. And the address they gave me was an address that I didn't know. And then they explained to me that they put people together in apartments-- lots of people-- for two years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was only then sent to Theresienstadt, and died in Theresienstadt in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she lived another two years in that horrible Vienna, without knowing where we are, what happened to us. She only knew that her husband died, and it must have been absolutely horrible. I only realized that last year, because my parents didn't talk to me about it. Do you know why they didn't leave with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, apparently, that they were too old, that they couldn't do it. I know they were in their 70s. And people then in their 70s didn't go on trips and things like people do now. And they never had traveled a lot. I think all they did is come from Poland to Vienna, and they-- they just couldn't face it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your grandfather perished in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how were his ashes preserved?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that-- the community told me that for the people who died, like that first year, in 1939, in Buchenwald, they sent back the ashes with their names to Vienna. And they were put in the Viennese Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: The authorities of Buchenwald were responsible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not just a friend who happened to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. It's just something they did. But they only did that for a very short time, because then it became so many people that they didn't bother with anything. I was very surprised to hear that they did that. But it was right in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother, was she also recovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother?

speaker: interviewer
text: She perished in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: She perished-- no, not at all. We don't even know exactly when. It's just that I realized that, after the war, I have a picture of their grave, that my uncle put the name of my grandfather, and the dates of his birth and his death. And my grandmother was just, like, added to it without any dates, because we didn't know exactly when it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you-- when first became aware of-- of what had happened in Europe? The extent of what-- of what had happened? Do you remember that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do remember it, yes. But at the same time, I was so involved in learning, and wanting to become a fashion creator, et cetera, and in living, that I-- I didn't pay too much attention to it. It didn't-- I just wanted to go on with my life. It was terribly important to me to do a lot of things, and to change, and not-- just because of that reason, it was very important for me to become my own person, to do things I wanted to do. And it really occu-- occupied me, totally. Just leave the past, and do something

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when you first did realize, did you real-- was-- was it told to you in its full extent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't think that I saw pictures of it. It was told to me, but I did not see those atrocious movies until much later. I did not. I mean, people just wanted to heal and get normal-- have a normal life. And that they-- they couldn't change anything at this point, so they just wanted to go on with their life. And, I mean, I did. I don't know about the others. I know a lot of people were traumatized. But I think that by wanting to do what I wanted to do, I found my expression.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you-- you wanted to be in Paris and to be part of the fashion world there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still had a sense that Europe could provide a home for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a sense that, in France, i could go on. I didn't realize how the French had acted with the Jewish people. I just put it all in one basket. I thought it was the Germans that did it-- the Germans, only the Nazis. I didn't realize that the French also helped a lot to put people into the [INAUDIBLE] and all that. I found out about this much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you had left France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your experience in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I-- I decided that, when I finished school, that what I wanted to do was, simply go to Paris and work in the maison de couture. As simple as that. So all I did-- I took all the addresses of all the marvelous places-- [FRENCH]-- and I decided that, at one point, I'll just go there and see what I can do. But before that, I went to Lyon for a certain amount of time and stayed with my parents. And I thought it was just awful to live in Lyon. It was just a small town. There were no possibilities. It wasn't, at all, the place I wanted to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked my father to lend me some money. And I said, I'm going to go for two weeks to Paris, and I'll see what I can do. If I succeed, fine. If I don't, I'll come back to Lyon. So my father gave me some money. And some friends told me about other friends they had in Paris, because I didn't know anybody. So I contacted-- I went to Paris, took a train, and contacted these people. I think I arrived on a Friday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were wonderful people. They were Germans from Leipzig-- lovely people that used to be bankers, and very well-to-do in Leipzig, in Germany. And they had three daughters. And at this point, they lived in a very small hotel in [INAUDIBLE]. And I took a room, I think, in that hotel, or maybe I slept with one of the girls in one room for one night. And they had in this little hotel room-- they had a [INAUDIBLE] shabbat. They had a real shabbat. They had challah. They had wine. They did that every Friday night with their children and with other friends. And this is how I started in Paris. And they were just-- for the rest of the time, they were like my family. They were absolutely wonderful. Their name is Krohl. And they then emigrated to Israel, but that's another story. So they were lovely to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started Monday morning. I decided, now I'm going to every place. And I took the subway-- the metro-- and started going from one place to another. I just said I'm-- this is my name. I just came out of art school in Geneva. I'm very interested in fashion. Is there anything I can do? And to the third place or fourth place I went, which was called [? Maggie Wolf, ?] which is a wonderful establishment in a wonderful house with a garden, next to the [INAUDIBLE] Champs-Elysees in the best part of Paris. They said, well, do you speak languages? I said, yes, I speak German, and I speak English. I spoke a little bit of English.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, well, maybe you could help our press attache, [INAUDIBLE], to help him with the press. And I said, sure, anything. So I met this very charming gentleman, and he engaged me to help him with the press. It had nothing to do with designing, but it was a very exciting job. And they said, when you start you need a black dress. I didn't have a black dress. So these girls of this family got together. One lent me a skirt. One lend me some kind of a tunic. And I had a black dress. And I started working there. It was very nice. There were lots of shows in Paris. It wasn't just one show, when you showed couture. It-- you showed through two months. You showed the press, and you showed private customers. But you had a show every single day with models. And it was wonderful. And I just helped in all kinds of capacities-- writing, seating people, whatever. I was part of it. And it was extraordinary. The good part about it was, also, that the mother of Monsieur [INAUDIBLE] was a fashion editor at a big magazine called And he introduced me to her, and after a few months of [? Maggie Wolf, ?] I started freelancing. And I got jobs through the magazine to go to different houses and just make fashion sketches, which they published there. Also, during that time, I went to sketch to an academy to learn how to sketch nudes. And there I met a wonderful person, who was Viennese like me and who had gone to America and come back to France and wanted to do the same thing as I did, fashion illustration and fashion creation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two of us got together, and she had a much nicer room than I, because she had come back from America and she had more means. She also had much nicer clothes. So what we did is we sketched each other. We made a whole portfolio of marvelous fashion sketches, she of me and I of her. Of course, I was very small, so was she, so we added the proportions of a fashion model.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used her furs, her scarves. We sat. We made poses. And we made a whole portfolio. Then we both had D Day. We went out to show it to magazines. And we both were accepted in magazines. I was in [INAUDIBLE], and she was in [INAUDIBLE]. And we got assignments. And these assignments were wonderful. We went to every maison de couture, and they made us sketch things. I still have the magazines here with my sketches. And this is what I really wanted to do. So I really started being very happy in Paris. I mean, materially, no, I didn't have-- I didn't have much money. I lived in a tiny hotel in [INAUDIBLE]. But I did what I wanted to do, and I was absolutely thrilled. In the meantime, PPP, my husband-to-be, who was also at the Ecole with me, got a visa from America, because his brother was in America. And he had to leave in 1947. Switzerland wouldn't hold him another minute, because he had a visa to go to America. We were both totally heartbroken, and he promised that he would come back to Europe and marry me. He left. I finished school, went to Paris, as I just explained. And he was in America. So every-- he wrote me a lot of letters. And then, of course, he'll tell you, he had a totally different life. He came to America. He felt totally out of place and couldn't come back to Europe right away. And when he finally had everything ready to go back to Europe, he was drafted for Korea. And four years had gone by. I went to Paris and stayed in Paris. I lived there for three years, and did all these things, and was very happy there. I really expected him to come back, and to also build an existence for himself as a cartoonist in France. But it never happened, because the Korean War happened, and he was drafted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me an affidavit to Paris, and I left for America to see him again. It made me very unhappy. I didn't want to come here. I had no intention, but it  was the only way for us to get together again. Paris was wonderful. It opened a whole world for me. And what is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul Peter Porges. PPP. I always knew him under PPP, because when he left Vienna as a little boy, alone with other children, his grandmother had knitted him a sweater. And she had put a round design with three Ps in the middle. And all these children that met him-- the ones from Berlin, the ones from all the other parts of Germany saw these three Ps, and they started calling him PPP. And that's how our-- all our old friends know him as PPP, and I know him like that too. That's how I met him. And so here I came to America, and started a whole life in a country that I didn't want to come to, and started all over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you had returned-- that you have returned to Vienna. What prompted that decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I returned to Vienna the first time, I think, in 1956. And the decision was made mostly because, at this point, the woman who took care of my husband was still alive-- his nurse, a-- a very wonderful woman. And he wanted to see Raizi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Israel, where my sister had settled in the meantime. We visited my sister in Israel, and on the way back, we stopped for a couple of days in Vienna, just to see Raizi, to see her again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as a matter of fact, Raizi, out of all the things that happened, kept a few things of the family. And she gave it to me. I have it here. She-- she gave me an old, beautiful coffee machine, a coffee-- where you do your coffee. A coffee mill from [? Mycene. ?] It's beautiful. It's wood, and blue and white porcelain. I have it in my kitchen. She gave me that. She gave me a few things that were still left from the family, which was wonderful. And she told us about a lot of things that happened, and how she was holding on to them during the war. And it was very important for us to see her. So that's why we went back to Vienna. And then we didn't go back for many, many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what became of your property?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. No idea. Just gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't attempt to go back and see your old home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We looked a little bit when we were in Vienna that time. But there was nothing left. Other people moved in, and-- we actually-- my husband's grandparents came from a small town near Vienna. And there, there was still-- the property was still left. There was a house. And people had moved into it. But that's another story, and I think my husband will tell you about it. We went back to that place at some point.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was never-- your parents never attempted to reclaim anything from Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't. But we didn't have a house, per se, in Vienna. We lived in an apartment. My grandmother lived in an apartment. So it's just the things that were inside that belonged to us, and all these things were gone. There was no-- no point. There was no hope to get anything back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in visiting the Archives in-- in the-- this old synagogue in Vienna, what have you been able to find there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was just able to find out what I told you about my grandparents. I was just absolutely amazed to find out that my poor grandmother still lived for two years in Vienna, without knowing what happened to anybody, only that her husband died. And with strangers. Definitely with strangers. And that two years after, they shipped her out to Theresienstadt. It's just incredible to me what she went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people who are maintaining the archives, it's-- they're a private organization, or is it run by the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's part of the city. I think so. But it's mostly Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about just more general-- more general things. This whole experience, the whole living through these years. Can-- can you begin to-- to say how-- how it's affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It affected me and my sister in a way that we went through so many-- after Vienna, we went through so many situations where we were in camps and make-do situations-- that we always had a-- an incredible yearning for beautiful things and beautiful surroundings, both of us. And whether it's clothes, silverware, a plate, a little vase-- we always looked for flowers, for the beautiful things in life. For art. For-- it was very important to us. Music, things like that, spiritual things. Very much. And we were terribly striving for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are-- are there things today-- are-- are there ways that this experience manifests itself within your daily-- within your daily experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was trying to bring up my children in this direction. I'm always trying to do things for people, whenever I can. To be part of it, to help. About six years ago, I had cancer. I had ovarian cancer, and I was very lucky to pull through. I must say, I had a pretty good attitude. I believed that I would be well again, and I am. I am very lucky. But I always try to help people who have that same experience. Even yesterday somebody called me-- tell them what happened, because people are so afraid of just the word, cancer; of the word, chemotherapy. You lose your hair. It's a terrible thing for a woman to lose her hair. I did. I mean, it just came out in bushels. It was a horrible thing. At the end, I didn't even dare comb my hair anymore. It was just a matted mess. And then I couldn't take it anymore, and I just decided to have it all cut off. And I was wearing mostly kerchiefs and sometimes wigs, because I-- after a while, I went back to my work. But people are so scared. So I always try to help them, to explain what really happens, and that you can come out of it. And I also always help people with their image. Like, I-- I know exactly what to wear to be elegant, and what suits you. And friends of mine had absolutely no idea. I would take them and go shopping with them and show them how they can do things, and look wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I've done that for men, for women. It's just a little thing, but I like to help people in many directions. This is just what I do best, OK? I-- I'm not very good nursing people and doing things like that, which I admire tremendously. But it's not my forte. So I do what I can. Let's speak about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children. I have two daughters. And it was absolutely incredible having children. It's the most wonderful experience. You have no idea what it is until you have them. And then they both grew up, practically, with a pencil in their hand because both their father and-- and me were always drawing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the children started drawing from a very early age. And Claudia, who is now-- she was doing children's books and collages. For some reason, she knew how to draw people and she always did very small things, but perfectly. Perfect proportions hands, feet everything-- She just could do it without any training or art school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Vivette did totally different things, but also very beautifully. So both children are very much talented for the arts. And they've gone in-- in that direction, both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandchildren-- one of them likes to draw. One is very small. There's a little girl that's going to be two. And my oldest one, Kitt, is interested in everything that's the internet, and all these things that I don't understand anything about. It gets very difficult for me to converse with him because of that. The only one thing-- I think he showed me something very exciting on his video. He said, I have classical music for you, Lula. They call me Lula. And he put on a little bit of Beethoven, a little bit of Schubert. He was so excited. He said, this is your-- your kind of thing, your kind of thing. I have it right here on my video. He was so excited about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could-- if you could communicate a single thought, a single-- a single theme to people who would watch this, about your experience and about the experience of the Jews in Europe, what-- what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: What would that be? Just to learn from it and to go on-- to go on with your life, and to do the best you can with your life. And-- and really, for me, it was a fantastic experience. I think all of the experience, going from one country to another was wonderful. I speak perfectly French, which means that French culture is very easy for me to grasp. I remember my German very well. So I can read German books, French books. I think it's such a wonderful thing to be able to do that. I appreciate that a great deal, and being able to have stayed alive, and to profit from it. It is absolutely fantastic. I think it's great

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you think is-- is the importance of-- of collecting these histories, what we're doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the importance is that people should realize what happened in somebody's life, how you can be pulled out of your life totally from one minute to the other. And what happens to you, and how it transforms you. And the problem is that-- I-- I think, after living all those years-- is that it happens all the time, all over the world, just in different parts. And that is probably part of people, the good and the bad. It just happens all over. And that it will go on happening, I think. It's just humanity. It's just the way it is. And we-- you, yourself, should try to do the best you can not to be evil. That's all. And to live with people. Communicate.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
